topic: Community Health Centre referral and follow-up
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with known hypertension is managed at a Community Health Centre (CHC). He presents with a 3-week history of progressive exertional dyspnoea and bilateral ankle oedema. His BP is 168/104 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, SpO2 94% on room air. Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly and bilateral perihilar haziness. He is started on a diuretic. The referring doctor at the CHC decides to continue managing him locally without referral to a district hospital for 2 more weeks to 'see how he responds'. Which of the following is the most likely consequence of this management decision?
options: The patient will achieve adequate BP control with the diuretic alone
Delayed referral risks progression to decompensated heart failure with acute pulmonary oedema
The patient's dyspnoea will resolve as diuresis reduces preload sufficiently
Bilateral ankle oedema will resolve, confirming simple venous insufficiency
The patient can be safely monitored monthly at CHC level given his stable vitals
explanation: This patient has signs of hypertensive heart failure with reduced SpO2 and radiological pulmonary oedema. CHCs are designed for first-contact care and defined referral criteria; uncontrolled hypertensive heart failure with hypoxia warrants urgent district hospital referral. Delaying referral risks acute decompensation and pulmonary oedema, a life-threatening complication.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A diuretic alone will not control hypertension at this severity; ACE inhibitors or ARBs with specialist input are needed.
Correct — delayed referral in decompensating heart failure risks acute pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure.
Diuresis may partially reduce preload, but the underlying hypertensive cardiomyopathy requires comprehensive management beyond CHC scope.
Bilateral ankle oedema with dyspnoea and cardiomegaly is cardiac in origin, not simple venous insufficiency.
SpO2 of 94% and radiological oedema indicate he is not stable; this exceeds CHC management capacity.
clinicalPearl: CHCs serve as gatekeepers; hypertensive heart failure with hypoxia and radiological pulmonary oedema requires prompt referral — delaying risks fatal decompensation.
keyClue: SpO2 94%, cardiomegaly, perihilar haziness on CXR, uncontrolled hypertension
commonExamTrap: Assuming diuresis alone is sufficient and that stable-looking vitals permit continued CHC management
missedConcept: Understanding the referral thresholds of primary-level facilities and complications of delayed referral in heart failure
redFlagReason: Decompensating heart failure with hypoxia — delayed referral can cause acute pulmonary oedema and death

sourceLecture: Consultation and Counselling in Family Medicine
sourceFile: Consultation and Counselling in Family Medicine.pdf
clinicalStem: A 44-year-old woman with Type 2 diabetes was counselled using a patient-centred approach 6 months ago. She was given information about dietary modification, self-monitoring of blood glucose, and the importance of metformin adherence. She returns today with HbA1c of 10.2% (was 8.1% at last visit), fasting glucose of 14.3 mmol/L, and complains of polyuria, polydipsia, and a non-healing ulcer on her right heel for 3 weeks. On examination, the ulcer is 2 cm, has a necrotic base, and there is reduced sensation to monofilament testing bilaterally. Which of the following best explains the clinical trajectory this patient is on?
options: Transient hyperglycaemia due to intercurrent illness, expected to self-resolve
Progressive diabetic peripheral neuropathy with high-risk foot ulcer indicating poor glycaemic control with risk of limb loss
Venous leg ulcer unrelated to her diabetes management
Metformin side effect causing osmotic symptoms and peripheral numbness
Normal progression of Type 2 diabetes unrelated to counselling adherence
explanation: Worsening HbA1c from 8.1% to 10.2% over 6 months with a necrotic heel ulcer and bilateral reduced monofilament sensation indicates diabetic peripheral neuropathy and a Wagner Grade 2+ diabetic foot ulcer, a direct complication of sustained poor glycaemic control. This trajectory carries a high risk of osteomyelitis and lower limb amputation. The clinical picture reflects failure to adhere to the counselled management plan.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The pattern of rising HbA1c, neuropathy signs, and a non-healing necrotic ulcer is not intercurrent illness — it represents a chronic complication trajectory.
Correct — progressive diabetic neuropathy with a high-risk foot ulcer secondary to poor glycaemic control, at risk of osteomyelitis and amputation.
Venous ulcers are typically above the medial malleolus with lipodermatosclerosis; a necrotic heel ulcer with neuropathy is a diabetic foot ulcer.
Metformin does not cause peripheral neuropathy or non-healing ulcers; hyperglycaemia itself causes both.
This is not normal progression — it reflects modifiable complications driven by non-adherence to a counselled management plan.
clinicalPearl: A rising HbA1c with monofilament loss and a necrotic foot ulcer signals a diabetic foot emergency requiring urgent multidisciplinary intervention to prevent amputation.
keyClue: Necrotic heel ulcer + reduced monofilament bilaterally + HbA1c worsening to 10.2%
commonExamTrap: Attributing the ulcer to venous disease or dismissing rising HbA1c as intercurrent illness
missedConcept: Longitudinal consequences of non-adherence identified during counselling follow-up visits — diabetic foot as a preventable complication
redFlagReason: Necrotic diabetic foot ulcer with neuropathy — risk of osteomyelitis, systemic sepsis, and limb loss if not urgently managed

topic: Complications of untreated dehydration in diarrhoeal disease
clinicalStem: A mother brings her 14-month-old child to the primary health clinic. The child has had watery diarrhoea for 4 days, vomited twice today, and has not urinated in the past 10 hours. On examination: the child is lethargic and unable to drink, sunken fontanelle, deeply sunken eyes, very dry mucous membranes, skin pinch returns very slowly (>2 seconds), HR 168 bpm, RR 42/min, capillary refill time 4 seconds. Weight today is 8.8 kg (usual weight 9.4 kg). Which of the following is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE next step at this primary health facility?
options: Start oral rehydration therapy (ORS) at 75 mL/kg over 4 hours and reassess
Administer IV Ringer's lactate 100 mL/kg over 3 hours as per WHO Plan C and arrange urgent referral
Encourage breastfeeding and give ORS by cup and spoon every 5 minutes
Give oral zinc supplementation and anti-diarrhoeal medication and reassess in 24 hours
Obtain stool culture and await results before initiating rehydration
explanation: This child has WHO-classified severe dehydration (lethargy, sunken eyes and fontanelle, very slow skin pinch, inability to drink, >10% weight loss, prolonged capillary refill). WHO Plan C requires immediate IV rehydration with Ringer's lactate 100 mL/kg over 3 hours in children and urgent referral if IV access or monitoring cannot be sustained at the primary facility. Oral ORS is contraindicated in a lethargic child unable to drink.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ORS Plan B (75 mL/kg over 4 hours) is for some dehydration, not severe dehydration; a lethargic child who cannot drink cannot safely receive oral fluids.
Correct — WHO Plan C IV rehydration with urgent referral is mandatory for severe dehydration with shock features.
Breastfeeding and slow ORS are appropriate for no or mild dehydration; this child is severely dehydrated and cannot swallow safely.
Zinc supplementation is an adjunct, not an emergency intervention; anti-diarrhoeals are contraindicated in young children; 24-hour delay would be fatal.
Rehydration must precede any investigation — waiting for culture results in a shocked child risks death from circulatory collapse.
clinicalPearl: Lethargy + inability to drink + very slow skin pinch = severe dehydration requiring immediate IV Plan C; ORS is contraindicated — refer urgently.
keyClue: Lethargic, unable to drink, very slow skin pinch, 10-hour anuria, >6% weight loss
commonExamTrap: Choosing oral ORS (Plan B) for a child who cannot drink and is lethargic — Plan C with IV fluids is the only safe option
missedConcept: WHO dehydration classification and the distinction between Plan B (oral) and Plan C (IV) based on conscious state and ability to drink
redFlagReason: Severe dehydration with shock features (prolonged CRT, lethargy, tachycardia) — circulatory collapse and death without immediate IV rehydration

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the primary health clinic by his grandmother. He was weaned at 6 months and has been on a predominantly maize-porridge diet. Examination reveals: bilateral pitting oedema of the feet and legs, sparse depigmented hair that pulls out easily, a 'flaky paint' skin rash over the buttocks and thighs, and a distended abdomen. He is irritable and anorexic. Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is 10.8 cm. Weight-for-height Z-score is -2.8. Which of the following complications is this child at HIGHEST risk of developing if untreated?
options: Iron-deficiency anaemia causing pallor and koilonychia
Rickets with bowing of legs due to vitamin D deficiency
Sepsis and hypoglycaemia due to severely impaired immune function and glycogen depletion
Pellagra with a photosensitive dermatitis rash in a sun-exposed distribution
Scurvy with bleeding gums and perifollicular haemorrhages
explanation: This child has kwashiorkor: bilateral oedema, skin changes ('flaky paint'), hair changes, and predominantly protein-deficient diet. Kwashiorkor causes profound immunosuppression and depleted glycogen stores, making sepsis (often from gram-negative organisms) and hypoglycaemia the leading causes of early death. MUAC <11.5 cm confirms severe acute malnutrition requiring urgent therapeutic feeding and infection screening.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Iron-deficiency anaemia is common but not the most immediately life-threatening complication in kwashiorkor; sepsis and hypoglycaemia kill first.
Rickets is caused by vitamin D deficiency, not protein deficiency; this child's clinical picture is kwashiorkor.
Correct — immunosuppression and glycogen depletion in kwashiorkor make sepsis and hypoglycaemia the primary causes of mortality.
Pellagra (niacin deficiency) causes a photosensitive rash in sun-exposed areas; the 'flaky paint' rash in kwashiorkor is distributed over oedematous areas, not sun-exposed skin.
Scurvy is caused by vitamin C deficiency; while possible in malnutrition, it is not the primary life-threatening complication here.
clinicalPearl: In kwashiorkor, sepsis and hypoglycaemia are the leading killers — always screen for infection and measure blood glucose on admission; give glucose before feeding.
keyClue: Bilateral pitting oedema + flaky paint rash + depigmented hair + maize-only diet + MUAC <11.5 cm
commonExamTrap: Confusing the 'flaky paint' rash of kwashiorkor with pellagra's photosensitive distribution, or focusing on anaemia rather than the life-threatening sepsis risk
missedConcept: Kwashiorkor causes severe immune dysfunction; infection and hypoglycaemia are the proximate causes of death, not the nutritional deficiency itself

subtopic: Complications of persistent diarrhoea in children
clinicalStem: A 19-month-old girl has had loose stools for 16 days. She was treated empirically with an antibiotic for 5 days without improvement. She now has 5–6 watery stools per day, has lost 0.8 kg since onset, and her mother reports poor appetite. Examination: MUAC 11.0 cm, mild dehydration signs, no blood in stool. Stool microscopy shows no ova or parasites. She has been exclusively formula-fed since 4 months. Which of the following is the most likely long-term consequence if this episode is not appropriately managed?
options: Full spontaneous recovery within 7 days as most diarrhoea is self-limiting
Development of acute appendicitis as a complication of prolonged intestinal inflammation
Persistent malnutrition and growth faltering due to enteropathy and ongoing nutrient malabsorption
Systemic lupus erythematosus triggered by intestinal immune activation
Lactose intolerance as a transient side effect of antibiotic use, resolving in 48 hours
explanation: Persistent diarrhoea (>14 days) causes secondary enteropathy — villous atrophy and brush border enzyme deficiency impair nutrient absorption. Combined with weight loss and a MUAC of 11.0 cm already at the severe acute malnutrition threshold, ongoing malabsorption will cause progressive growth faltering and malnutrition. WHO classifies persistent diarrhoea as a nutritional emergency requiring catch-up feeding protocols.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Persistent diarrhoea by definition has already lasted >14 days; at 16 days with weight loss and mucosal damage, spontaneous resolution without intervention is unlikely.
Appendicitis is not a complication of persistent diarrhoeal enteropathy and is unrelated to this clinical picture.
Correct — secondary enteropathy from persistent diarrhoea causes villous atrophy and ongoing malabsorption, leading to malnutrition and growth faltering.
Systemic lupus is an autoimmune disease unrelated to persistent infectious or post-infectious diarrhoea in a toddler.
While antibiotic-associated diarrhoea exists, 16 days of diarrhoea with weight loss and MUAC at SAM threshold is not explained by antibiotic use alone, and this does not resolve in 48 hours.
clinicalPearl: Persistent diarrhoea (>14 days) causes secondary enteropathy and villous atrophy — the nutritional consequence of ongoing malabsorption is the major long-term harm.
keyClue: 16 days of diarrhoea, 0.8 kg weight loss, MUAC 11.0 cm, no response to antibiotics
commonExamTrap: Assuming self-resolution because no pathogen was found on stool microscopy — persistent diarrhoea itself damages the gut regardless of aetiology
missedConcept: Persistent diarrhoea is a distinct WHO category (>14 days) associated with secondary mucosal damage and malnutrition risk independent of the original pathogen

topic: General practice continuity of care and chronic disease follow-up
clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with a 10-year history of hypertension has been attending a general practice clinic irregularly. He missed his last 3 follow-up appointments. He now presents with a sudden onset severe headache ('worst of my life'), neck stiffness, and photophobia. BP on arrival is 212/118 mmHg. GCS is 14/15 (confused). CT head is unavailable at this facility. Which of the following is the most important immediate action?
options: Administer oral nifedipine 10 mg to lower BP urgently before transfer
Perform lumbar puncture immediately to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage
Stabilise airway, establish IV access, and arrange immediate emergency transfer to a facility with neurosurgical capacity
Give oral analgesia and reassess in 2 hours to determine if headache is tension-type
Start IV labetalol to achieve a BP of 120/80 mmHg within 1 hour
explanation: A 'worst of my life' thunderclap headache with neck stiffness, photophobia, hypertensive emergency (BP 212/118), and altered GCS is highly suspicious for subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) — a neurosurgical emergency. Without CT capability, the priority is airway protection, IV access, and immediate emergency transfer. Oral nifedipine causes precipitous BP drops that worsen cerebral perfusion in SAH; LP should only follow CT to exclude raised ICP; and analgesia with a watch-and-wait approach is dangerous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies — it causes uncontrolled BP drops and can worsen cerebral ischaemia in SAH.
Lumbar puncture before CT risks brainstem herniation if there is raised intracranial pressure — CT must precede LP, and CT is unavailable here.
Correct — ABCDE approach with immediate emergency transfer to a neurosurgical centre is the only safe option when SAH is suspected and CT is unavailable.
A 'worst of my life' thunderclap headache is a red flag requiring emergency evaluation, not a watch-and-wait approach — this could be fatal.
Aggressive BP lowering to 120/80 mmHg in SAH risks reducing cerebral perfusion pressure and worsening outcome; BP targets in SAH are institution-specific and managed under specialist guidance.
clinicalPearl: Thunderclap headache + neck stiffness + altered consciousness in a hypertensive patient = subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise — transfer immediately, do not delay with oral agents.
keyClue: Thunderclap headache + neck stiffness + photophobia + BP 212/118 + GCS 14
commonExamTrap: Giving oral nifedipine or performing LP before CT — both are dangerous in suspected SAH
missedConcept: The thunderclap headache is a red flag that mandates emergency transfer; at primary care level, stabilise and transfer — do not investigate or treat beyond ABCDE
redFlagReason: Suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage — thunderclap headache with meningism and altered GCS in hypertensive emergency; delay in transfer risks death

sourceLecture: Consultation and Counselling in Family Medicine
sourceFile: Consultation and Counselling in Family Medicine.pdf
topic: Breaking bad news and follow-up counselling
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman was told by her family physician 2 weeks ago that her biopsy confirmed breast cancer requiring mastectomy and chemotherapy. She returns today saying she has decided to stop all conventional treatment and pursue 'herbal cleansing'. She says she was given the diagnosis too quickly and never given a chance to ask questions. She is tearful and states she 'just wants to feel in control'. Using a patient-centred counselling framework, which of the following responses BEST addresses her immediate needs?
options: Inform her that stopping treatment is her legal right and discharge her from care
Explain that herbal remedies have no evidence base and insist she proceeds with surgery this week
Acknowledge her feelings, explore her concerns and understanding, and negotiate a plan that respects autonomy while clearly explaining the risks of treatment delay
Refer her immediately to psychiatry for evaluation of treatment refusal
Contact her family without her consent to persuade them to encourage compliance
explanation: Patient-centred counselling as taught in family medicine requires exploring the patient's ideas, concerns, and expectations (ICE) before negotiating. This patient is expressing a loss of control — a psychosocial response to how the diagnosis was delivered. Acknowledging her feelings, correcting misconceptions, providing information she missed, and offering a collaborative plan respects autonomy while ensuring informed decision-making. Coercion, dismissal, or bypassing the patient violates core principles.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While autonomy exists, discharging without addressing her unmet informational and emotional needs is abandonment and clinically dangerous — she has a curable cancer.
Confrontational insistence on surgery dismisses her feelings and violates patient-centred communication; it will entrench her refusal.
Correct — the ICE model (ideas, concerns, expectations) is the framework; acknowledge, explore, inform, and negotiate collaboratively.
Disagreeing with a treatment plan based on emotional distress and poor information is not a psychiatric emergency; it is a counselling need.
Contacting family without consent violates patient confidentiality and autonomy — this is both unethical and illegal without her permission.
clinicalPearl: Treatment refusal often signals unmet informational or emotional needs — the patient-centred approach (ICE model) resolves most refusals better than coercion.
keyClue: Patient says she 'never had a chance to ask questions' and 'wants to feel in control' — informational and autonomy gap, not irrational refusal
commonExamTrap: Treating the refusal as psychiatric or overriding autonomy by involving family without consent
missedConcept: Patient-centred counselling addresses the patient's agenda (ICE) before the clinician's agenda — most apparent 'refusals' dissolve with proper communication

subtopic: Electrolyte complications of profuse watery diarrhoea
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old fisherman presents to a rural health post with a 12-hour history of profuse, painless 'rice-water' stools (estimated 12 litres output) and vomiting. He is afebrile, BP 78/50 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, skin turgor very poor, extremities cold. He is too weak to sit up but is still conscious. Blood sent to district lab returns: Na+ 128 mmol/L, K+ 2.8 mmol/L, HCO3- 10 mmol/L. Which complication is the most immediate threat to this patient's life?
options: Hyponatraemic seizures due to rapidly falling serum sodium
Hypokalaemia-induced cardiac arrhythmia compounded by metabolic acidosis causing cardiovascular collapse
Acute renal failure due to contrast nephropathy from IV fluids
Aspiration pneumonia due to repeated vomiting
Hypoglycaemia due to starvation from 12 hours without food
explanation: This is cholera with severe dehydration and metabolic acidosis (HCO3- 10 mmol/L) and hypokalaemia (K+ 2.8 mmol/L). Hypokalaemia in the context of acidosis is profoundly dangerous: as acidosis corrects with rehydration, K+ shifts intracellularly and the true deficit becomes greater, precipitating fatal ventricular arrhythmias. Combined with haemodynamic shock (BP 78/50, cold extremities), cardiovascular collapse from arrhythmia is the most immediate threat. Aggressive IV Ringer's lactate with K+ replacement is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Na+ at 128 mmol/L is hyponatraemic but not at a level causing acute seizures in this clinical context; the cardiovascular threat from hypokalaemia and shock is more immediate.
Correct — hypokalaemia compounded by metabolic acidosis in hypovolaemic shock creates the highest immediate risk of fatal ventricular arrhythmia.
Contrast nephropathy requires iodinated contrast agent; IV rehydration fluids (Ringer's lactate or normal saline) do not cause nephropathy.
Aspiration is a risk but is secondary; the cardiovascular collapse from shock and arrhythmia is the immediate life threat.
Hypoglycaemia is not the primary concern in a previously well adult after 12 hours; electrolyte-driven arrhythmia in severe shock is the priority.
clinicalPearl: In cholera, hypokalaemia masked by acidosis is a hidden killer — when acidosis corrects with rehydration, K+ falls further; always replace potassium alongside aggressive IV rehydration.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on hyponatraemia when the more dangerous complication is hypokalaemic arrhythmia in the context of metabolic acidosis and shock
missedConcept: Acidosis masks the severity of hypokalaemia — as pH corrects, K+ falls further and fatal arrhythmias ensue; K+ replacement must accompany rehydration
redFlagReason: Hypovolaemic shock with metabolic acidosis and hypokalaemia — risk of fatal ventricular arrhythmia and cardiovascular collapse without immediate IV rehydration and K+ replacement

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old office worker presents for a routine check-up. He has no symptoms. BMI is 34 kg/m², waist circumference 108 cm. BP 138/88 mmHg. Fasting glucose 6.3 mmol/L (normal <6.1). Fasting triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, LDL 3.4 mmol/L. He is a non-smoker, drinks alcohol socially. He is reassured by his previous doctor that 'a little weight is normal at your age'. Which of the following BEST describes his current condition and prognosis if lifestyle changes are not made?
options: Isolated hypertension requiring antihypertensive therapy alone; dietary changes are optional
Metabolic syndrome with high 10-year cardiovascular risk and high likelihood of progression to Type 2 diabetes and major adverse cardiovascular events
Pre-diabetes only, which is reversible and carries no significant cardiovascular risk independently
Borderline dyslipidaemia of no clinical significance given his asymptomatic status
Normal metabolic variation for his age group; no intervention required at this time
explanation: This patient meets criteria for metabolic syndrome: central obesity (waist >102 cm in men), elevated BP, impaired fasting glucose, hypertriglyceridaemia, and low HDL. Metabolic syndrome confers a 2-3 fold increased risk of cardiovascular events and a 5-fold increased risk of Type 2 diabetes. Without lifestyle intervention, progression to overt Type 2 diabetes within 5–10 years and a first cardiovascular event within 10–15 years is highly probable. Reassurance without intervention is harmful.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The full constellation of findings (central obesity, impaired fasting glucose, dyslipidaemia, hypertension) constitutes metabolic syndrome — treating hypertension alone ignores the broader risk.
Correct — metabolic syndrome is present with multiple risk factors converging to dramatically increase cardiovascular and diabetes risk.

Pre-diabetes in the context of metabolic syndrome carries substantial cardiovascular risk beyond glucose alone; the cardiovascular risk from hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and central obesity is additive and significant.
Hypertriglyceridaemia 2.8 mmol/L with low HDL 0.8 mmol/L is not borderline — it is part of atherogenic dyslipidaemia with significant cardiovascular risk.
This patient has five components of metabolic syndrome — this is not normal variation and warrants urgent lifestyle intervention and close follow-up.
clinicalPearl: Metabolic syndrome = 3 or more of: central obesity, elevated BP, high triglycerides, low HDL, impaired fasting glucose — confers 2-3x cardiovascular risk and 5x diabetes risk.
keyClue: Waist 108 cm + IFG + hypertriglyceridaemia + low HDL + BP 138/88 = metabolic syndrome
commonExamTrap: Reassuring an asymptomatic patient with 'borderline' values separately when together they constitute metabolic syndrome with high composite risk
missedConcept: Metabolic syndrome is defined by the clustering of risk factors, not their individual severity — the combined risk is multiplicative, not additive

topic: ORS use and monitoring — follow-up after rehydration
subtopic: Failure to respond to ORS — when to escalate
clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is treated with ORS (WHO Plan B: 75 mL/kg over 4 hours) for some dehydration due to acute watery diarrhoea. After 4 hours he has received the prescribed volume. Reassessment shows: still has sunken eyes, skin pinch returns slowly, is less alert than at arrival, has not urinated during the 4-hour period, and is now vomiting with each attempt to drink. Stool frequency has increased to 8 stools in the past hour. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Repeat another round of WHO Plan B ORS over the next 4 hours
Discharge home with ORS sachets and review in 48 hours
Add antibiotic therapy and continue oral rehydration
Classify as severe dehydration, initiate WHO Plan C IV rehydration, and arrange urgent referral
Give anti-emetic medication to allow continued oral rehydration
explanation: Failure to improve after WHO Plan B, with worsening clinical signs (less alert, persistent dehydration signs, no urine output, increasing stool frequency, inability to drink due to vomiting), represents deterioration and reclassification to severe dehydration. WHO guidelines specify that failure of oral rehydration with clinical deterioration mandates immediate transition to Plan C (IV Ringer's lactate) and urgent referral. Continuing oral fluids in a vomiting, deteriorating child risks aspiration and further deterioration.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Repeating Plan B is indicated only if reassessment shows improvement; this child has deteriorated — Plan B has failed.
Discharging a child with worsening dehydration signs and inability to drink is dangerous and constitutes medical negligence.
Antibiotics are not first-line for acute watery diarrhoea (usually viral or toxigenic); the priority here is haemodynamic stabilisation, not antimicrobials.
Correct — clinical deterioration after Plan B mandates reclassification to severe dehydration and immediate Plan C with urgent referral.
Anti-emetics in young children have significant adverse effects (extrapyramidal reactions with metoclopramide) and do not address the severity of dehydration; IV access is the safe solution.
clinicalPearl: Reassess after every Plan B course — worsening signs after oral rehydration = severe dehydration requiring IV Plan C; do not repeat a failed oral plan.
keyClue: Worsening alertness + no urine output after 4-hour Plan B + vomiting preventing oral intake + increased stool frequency
commonExamTrap: Repeating Plan B or adding an antibiotic rather than recognising clinical deterioration as requiring escalation to Plan C
missedConcept: WHO diarrhoea management requires reassessment after each plan — failure of Plan B with deterioration mandates immediate escalation to Plan C, not repetition
redFlagReason: Deteriorating dehydration with reduced consciousness and inability to drink — continued oral therapy risks aspiration; IV Plan C is urgently needed

subtopic: Iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy — fetal and maternal consequences
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation attends her antenatal visit. She has been non-adherent with iron supplementation for the past 8 weeks, citing nausea. She has pallor, Hb 7.8 g/dL (normal in pregnancy >11 g/dL), MCV 68 fL, MCH 22 pg. She is currently asymptomatic at rest. The community health worker had advised her that 'a little anaemia in pregnancy is normal and harmless'. Which of the following best describes the fetal complication she and her baby are at HIGHEST risk of?
options: Neonatal jaundice due to haemolytic disease of the newborn
Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), preterm delivery, and impaired neonatal iron stores leading to cognitive developmental delay
Neonatal hypercalcaemia due to impaired placental calcium transport
Congenital heart disease due to anaemia-related cardiac teratogenicity in the first trimester
Gestational diabetes developing in the mother secondary to iron deficiency
explanation: Severe iron-deficiency anaemia (Hb 7.8 g/dL) in pregnancy reduces oxygen delivery to the placenta, causing IUGR and increasing preterm delivery risk. The fetus accumulates iron stores primarily in the third trimester; maternal iron deficiency in this period depletes fetal iron stores, impairing myelination and neurotransmitter synthesis, which is associated with measurable cognitive and motor developmental delay in the neonate and infant.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Haemolytic disease of the newborn is caused by Rh or ABO incompatibility, not iron deficiency.
Correct — IUGR, preterm delivery, and impaired fetal iron stores leading to cognitive developmental delay are the principal fetal consequences of severe maternal iron deficiency.
Hypercalcaemia in the neonate is unrelated to maternal iron deficiency; calcium transport is a separate physiological process.
Iron deficiency does not cause cardiac teratogenicity; congenital heart disease arises from first-trimester structural abnormalities unrelated to haemoglobin levels.
Gestational diabetes is caused by insulin resistance, not iron deficiency, and is not a complication of anaemia.
clinicalPearl: Third-trimester iron deficiency depletes fetal iron stores essential for brain myelination — the cognitive consequences for the child are long-term and partially irreversible.
keyClue: Hb 7.8 g/dL at 32 weeks + non-adherent to iron + microcytic anaemia (MCV 68) = severe iron deficiency at the critical fetal iron-loading trimester
commonExamTrap: Accepting that 'mild anaemia is normal in pregnancy' and missing the severe maternal anaemia threshold and its fetal cognitive consequences
missedConcept: Fetal iron stores are built in the third trimester; maternal iron deficiency at this stage compromises neonatal brain iron with lasting cognitive effects

topic: General practice and preventive care — missed follow-up
clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman presents to her general practice clinic with a 4-month history of post-coital bleeding and intermittent foul-smelling vaginal discharge. She has not had a Pap smear in 12 years, despite two documented recall invitations sent by the clinic. She is multiparous (4 children), was sexually active from age 15, and has a history of HPV-related genital warts treated 18 years ago. Pelvic examination reveals an irregular, friable cervical lesion. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Repeat Pap smear today and recall her in 6 months for results
Reassure her that post-coital bleeding is commonly caused by a cervical ectropion and treat with silver nitrate
Refer urgently to gynaecological oncology for colposcopy and biopsy; this presentation is suspicious for invasive cervical carcinoma
Start a 2-week course of metronidazole for bacterial vaginosis and review
Screen for sexually transmitted infections and treat with dual antibiotic therapy
explanation: An irregular, friable cervical lesion with post-coital bleeding and offensive discharge in a woman with multiple cervical cancer risk factors (early coitus, multiparity, prior HPV infection, 12-year screening gap) is invasive cervical carcinoma until proven otherwise. A Pap smear is not adequate for a visible lesion — direct biopsy under colposcopy is required. Delay, reassurance, or treating empirically risks missing an advanced cancer and worsening prognosis. Urgent gynaecological oncology referral is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A Pap smear is a screening tool for asymptomatic women — in a woman with a visible suspicious lesion, it is inadequate; biopsy is required and any delay worsens prognosis.
Silver nitrate treats a benign ectropion; an irregular, friable lesion with this risk profile and symptom duration is not reassuringly benign — empirical treatment would dangerously delay diagnosis.
Correct — urgent gynaecological oncology referral for colposcopy and biopsy is mandatory; this presentation has a high pre-test probability of invasive cervical carcinoma.
Metronidazole treats anaerobic infection; while an offensive discharge may coexist with infection, treating empirically without investigating a visible suspicious lesion is dangerous.
STI screening is appropriate but secondary; the priority is biopsy of the suspicious lesion — STI treatment does not address the primary diagnostic concern.
clinicalPearl: A visible, irregular, friable cervical lesion is a 2-week wait cancer referral — do not perform a Pap smear; go directly to colposcopy and biopsy.
keyClue: Irregular friable cervical lesion + post-coital bleeding + 12-year screening gap + prior HPV + multiparity
commonExamTrap: Performing a Pap smear as the next step — a Pap smear is only for screening asymptomatic women; a visible lesion requires biopsy
missedConcept: Pap smear is a screening tool, not a diagnostic tool — any visible suspicious cervical lesion requires urgent colposcopy and biopsy, not cytology
redFlagReason: Highly suspicious invasive cervical carcinoma — delay in diagnosis at this stage significantly worsens survival; urgent oncology referral is mandatory
